scenario_config.name: overload_recovery
_dataset_notes: national_medium scale — matches endurance_steady so the NFR-1 baseline
recovery target (p95 < 1000 ms) is comparable across the two reports.
_traffic_notes: Four-stage profile (handled by benchmarks/k6/overload_recovery.js):
1.  2 min ramp 0 -> 22 RPS  (warmup, also stresses the buffer fill rate)
2.  5 min hold at 22 RPS    (2x the NFR-1 steady-state target — overload)
3.  1 s   snap drop -> 11 RPS
4.  5 min hold at 11 RPS    (recovery to NFR-1 baseline rate)
duration=12m is informational; the k6 stages above are authoritative.
Pass criteria (NFR-5):
- During phase 4, p95 returns below 1000 ms within 2 min of the drop,
- Redis ingest_buffer drains to 0 within the 5-min recovery window,
- System is stable (no creeping memory, no growing backend RSS).
Drops and 5xx ARE expected during phase 2. That is the overload signal.
What the thesis claims is recovery, not steady-state survival at 2x.
Post-processing tips:
- The phase-4 start timestamp is (ramp + hold + drop) seconds after the
k6 setup completes; the report records this implicitly via the stage
durations above.
- Walk http_req_duration metric points after the phase-4 boundary; the
first sample window where p95 < 1000 ms is the recovery-time number.
- resource_summary in the report exposes runtime_docker_stats; the
backend CPU dip and the flusher drain visibly trail the load drop.
VU sizing follows the lesson_bell convention: pre-allocated VUs scale to
the PEAK rate so VU contention does not muddy phase-2 readings.
